stem: Consider the point \( P = ([[x1]], [[y1]]) \) and the line \( [[a]]x + [[b]]y = [[c]] \). Find the point on the line closest to \( P \). What is the square of the minimum distance from \( P \) to the line?
solution: The squared distance is minimized where \( (x - [[x1]], y - [[y1]]) \) is parallel to the normal \( ([[a]], [[b]]) \). The classic result gives \( \dfrac{([[a]]([[x1]]) + [[b]]([[y1]]) - [[c]]))^{2}}{[[a]]^{2} + [[b]]^{2}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the squared numerator \( (ax_1 + by_1 - c)^{2} \) without dividing by \( a^{2}+b^{2} \).
This is the magnitude of \( ax_1 + by_1 - c \), missing both the square and the division.
This is only \( a^{2}+b^{2} \), the denominator of the distance formula.
This doubles the correct squared distance.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Consider the point \( P = ([[p]], [[q]]) \) and the circle \( x^{2} + y^{2} = [[r]]^{2} \) centered at the origin. Find the point on the circle farthest from \( P \). What is the square of that maximum distance?
solution: The point on the circle farthest from \( P \) lies on the ray from \( P \) through the origin, on the opposite side of the circle from \( P \). Its distance from \( P \) is \( |OP| + r = [[h]] + [[r]] \) (since \( |OP| = \sqrt{[[p]]^{2}+[[q]]^{2}} = [[h]] \)), so the squared maximum distance is \( ([[h]] + [[r]])^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( |OP| - r \), which gives the squared MINIMUM distance to the circle, not the maximum.
This forgets to square the sum \( |OP| + r \).
This adds the squares of \( |OP| \) and \( r \) instead of squaring their sum.
This shifts only the \( x \)-coordinate of \( P \) by \( r \) rather than moving along the ray through the origin.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Consider the point \( P = ([[x1]], [[y1]], [[z1]]) \) and the plane \( [[a]]x + [[b]]y + [[c]]z = [[d]] \) in three-dimensional space. Find the point on the plane closest to \( P \). What is the square of the minimum distance from \( P \) to the plane?
solution: The squared distance is minimized where \( (x - [[x1]], y - [[y1]], z - [[z1]]) \) is parallel to the plane's normal \( ([[a]], [[b]], [[c]]) \). The classic result gives \( \dfrac{([[a]]([[x1]]) + [[b]]([[y1]]) + [[c]]([[z1]]) - [[d]]))^{2}}{[[a]]^{2} + [[b]]^{2} + [[c]]^{2}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the squared numerator without dividing by \( a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}\).
This is the magnitude of the numerator, missing both the square and the division.
This is only \( a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}\), the denominator of the distance formula.
This doubles the correct squared distance.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Consider the point \( P = ([[px]], [[py]], [[pz]]) \) and the line \( L \) in three-dimensional space given parametrically by \( (x, y, z) = ([[x0]] + [[dx]]t,\ [[y0]] + [[dy]]t,\ [[z0]] + [[dz]]t) \). Minimize the squared distance from \( P \) to a point on \( L \) over all \( t \). What is this minimum squared distance?
solution: Writing \( \mathbf{w} = P - ([[x0]],[[y0]],[[z0]]) \) and \( \mathbf{d} = \langle [[dx]],[[dy]],[[dz]] \rangle \), calculus (\( d(D^{2})/dt = 0 \)) gives the minimum squared distance \( \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^{2} - \dfrac{(\mathbf{w}\cdot\mathbf{d})^{2}}{\lVert \mathbf{d} \rVert^{2}} = [[answer]] \), the standard point-to-line projection formula.
distractor_rationales: This is just \( \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^{2} \), the squared distance to the line's reference point \( ([[x0]],[[y0]],[[z0]]) \) itself, not the minimum over the whole line.
This is only the projection term, without subtracting it from \( \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^{2} \).
This is only \( \lVert \mathbf{d} \rVert^{2} \), the squared length of the direction vector.
This forgets to square the dot product \( \mathbf{w}\cdot\mathbf{d} \) before dividing.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: A relay station sits at the point \( P = ([[x1]], [[y1]]) \) on a map, and a straight highway follows the line \( y = [[m]]x + ([[b]]) \). An engineer wants to run the shortest possible cable from the station to the highway. Using calculus (or Lagrange multipliers) to minimize the squared distance from \( P \) to a point \( (x, y) \) on the highway, what is the square of the length of the shortest cable?
solution: Writing the highway as \( [[m]]x - y = -[[b]] \), the squared distance from \( P \) is minimized where \( (x-[[x1]], y-[[y1]]) \) is parallel to the normal \( ([[m]], -1) \). The classic point-to-line formula gives \( \dfrac{([[m]]([[x1]]) - ([[y1]]) + [[b]])^{2}}{[[m]]^{2}+1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the squared numerator alone, without dividing by \( m^{2}+1 \).
This is the (unsquared) magnitude of the numerator, missing both the square and the division.
This is only \( m^{2}+1 \), the denominator of the distance formula.
This squares the denominator as well as the numerator instead of leaving the denominator to the first power.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Consider the point \( P = ([[px]], [[py]], [[pz]]) \) and the sphere \( x^{2} + y^{2} + z^{2} = [[r]]^{2} \) centered at the origin. Find the point on the sphere farthest from \( P \). What is the square of that maximum distance?
solution: The point on the sphere farthest from \( P \) lies on the ray from \( P \) through the origin, on the opposite side of the sphere. Its distance from \( P \) is \( |OP| + r \), where \( |OP| = \sqrt{[[px]]^{2}+[[py]]^{2}+[[pz]]^{2}} = \sqrt{[[h2]]} \), so the squared maximum distance is \( (\sqrt{[[h2]]} + [[r]])^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( |OP| - r \), which gives the squared MINIMUM distance to the sphere, not the maximum.
This adds the squares of \( |OP| \) and \( r \) instead of squaring their sum.
This forgets to square the sum \( |OP| + r \).
This has a sign error in the expansion of the square.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Consider the point \( P = ([[p]], [[q]]) \) and the circle \( x^{2} + y^{2} = [[r]]^{2} \) centered at the origin. Find the point on the circle closest to \( P \) (the circle itself, not the disk it bounds). What is that minimum distance (not squared)?
solution: The closest point on the circle to \( P \) lies on the ray from the origin through \( P \) (or its extension). Since \( |OP| = \sqrt{[[p]]^{2}+[[q]]^{2}} = [[h]] \), the minimum distance from \( P \) to the circle is \( \lvert |OP| - r \rvert = \lvert [[h]] - [[r]] \rvert = [[answer]] \) (this handles both the case where \( P \) is outside the circle and where it is inside).
distractor_rationales: This is \( |OP| + r \), the distance to the FARTHEST point on the circle, not the closest.
This squares the distance instead of leaving it as a plain length.
This drops the absolute value, giving a negative result when \( P \) is inside the circle.
This has the subtraction backwards, giving a negative result when \( P \) is outside the circle.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
